questions.question:
Assume that source S and destination D are connected through two intermediate routers labeled R. Determine how many times each packet has to visit the network layer and the data link layer during a transmission from S to D.

A. Network layer - 4 times and Data link layer - 4 times
B. Network layer - 4 times and Data link layer - 3 times
C. Network layer - 4 times and Data link layer - 6 times
D. Network layer - 2 times and Data link layer - 6 times
questions.answer: C. Network layer - 4 times and Data link layer - 6 times
questions.to_find: Number of times each packet visits the network layer and the data link layer from S to D through two intermediate routers

understand.plain: A packet travels from source S to destination D through two intermediate routers R1 and R2, making the path: S -> R1 -> R2 -> D. At each node the packet is processed at various OSI layers. The network layer handles routing decisions and the data link layer handles frame transmission on each hop. We need to count how many times each layer is visited.

keywords.explain: Layer 3 in the OSI model responsible for logical addressing and routing. Every router and the source/destination process packets at this layer.
keywords.example: IP is the most common network layer protocol; every router reads the IP header to decide where to forward the packet.

keywords.explain: Layer 2 responsible for framing and reliable transmission between two directly connected nodes (one hop). It is visited at both the sending and receiving end of each physical link.
keywords.example: Ethernet frames are created/read at the data link layer on each hop between adjacent devices.

keywords.explain: A single link segment between two adjacent network devices. Each hop requires the data link layer to encapsulate/decapsulate a frame.
keywords.example: S to R1 is one hop; R1 to R2 is another hop; R2 to D is a third hop.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 620 160' width='620' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='50' width='80' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='50' y='84' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S</text><rect x='170' y='50' width='80' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='84' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R1</text><rect x='330' y='50' width='80' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='370' y='84' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R2</text><rect x='490' y='50' width='80' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='530' y='84' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D</text><line x1='90' y1='80' x2='170' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='250' y1='80' x2='330' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='410' y1='80' x2='490' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><text x='130' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>hop 1</text><text x='290' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>hop 2</text><text x='450' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>hop 3</text><text x='50' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>NL+DL</text><text x='210' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>NL+DL</text><text x='370' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>NL+DL</text><text x='530' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>NL+DL</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Path: S -- hop1 -- R1 -- hop2 -- R2 -- hop3 -- D. Network layer is visited at S, R1, R2, D (4 times). Data link layer is visited at both ends of each of the 3 hops (6 times total).

given.aim: Count visits to network layer and data link layer for a packet traversing S -> R1 -> R2 -> D

terms.example: S-R1 is link 1, R1-R2 is link 2, R2-D is link 3
terms.connects: Each node processes the network layer; each link endpoint processes the data link layer

terms.meaning: Processed at every node that reads or forwards the IP packet: S (originate), R1 (forward), R2 (forward), D (consume)
terms.connects: Every router and end host must read the IP header to route or receive the packet

terms.meaning: On each hop, the data link layer is used TWICE: once at the sending node (encapsulate frame) and once at the receiving node (decapsulate frame)
terms.example: 3 hops x 2 = 6 data link layer visits
terms.connects: Each physical link boundary requires frame creation and frame reading

given.plan: Count the number of nodes for network layer visits (4: S, R1, R2, D). Count the number of hops multiplied by 2 for data link layer visits (3 hops x 2 = 6). The correct option is the one matching 4 and 6.

steps.title: Identify the network topology
steps.formula_raw: Nodes = S, R1, R2, D; Links = S-R1, R1-R2, R2-D
steps.apply: The path from S to D is: S -> R1 -> R2 -> D. There are 4 nodes (including source and destination) and 3 links (hops).
steps.note: Two intermediate routers means the path has 4 nodes total.

steps.title: Count network layer visits
steps.apply: The network layer is processed at every node that handles the IP packet. S creates the packet (NL), R1 routes it (NL), R2 routes it (NL), D receives it (NL). Total = 4 visits.
steps.note: Routers are layer-3 devices so they always process the network layer.

steps.title: Count data link layer visits
steps.apply: On hop S->R1: S sends (DL) and R1 receives (DL) = 2 visits. On hop R1->R2: R1 sends (DL) and R2 receives (DL) = 2 visits. On hop R2->D: R2 sends (DL) and D receives (DL) = 2 visits. Total = 6 visits.
steps.note: The data link layer processes frames at both the transmitter and receiver of each link, not just one side.

solution.result: Network layer is visited 4 times and Data link layer is visited 6 times. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: OSI layer processing rule: network layer is visited at every node; data link layer is visited at both ends of every hop (link), giving 2 x (number of hops) total visits.

questions.question:
In the following pairs of OSI protocol layer/sub-layer and its functionality, the INCORRECT pair is:

A. Network layer and Routing
B. Data Link Layer and Bit synchronization
C. Transport layer and End-to-end process communication
D. Medium Access Control sub-layer and Channel sharing
questions.answer: B. Data Link Layer and Bit synchronization
questions.to_find: The INCORRECT pairing of an OSI layer/sub-layer with its functionality

understand.plain: This question asks about the OSI model's layers and their correct functionalities. One of the four listed pairs is incorrect — the functionality given does not belong to the stated layer. Bit synchronization is a function of the Physical layer (Layer 1), not the Data Link layer (Layer 2).

keywords.explain: The process of ensuring the sender and receiver agree on the timing of individual bits. This is a Physical layer (Layer 1) responsibility.
keywords.example: Manchester encoding is used at the physical layer to embed clock information within the data signal for bit synchronization.

keywords.explain: Handles framing, error detection/correction, flow control, and MAC addressing between adjacent nodes. Does NOT handle bit synchronization.
keywords.example: Ethernet protocol at Layer 2 adds source/destination MAC addresses to frames.

keywords.explain: A sub-layer of the Data Link layer that controls how devices on a shared network medium gain access to the channel.
keywords.example: CSMA/CD used in Ethernet is a MAC protocol for channel sharing.

understand.visual_alt: OSI Layer diagram: Layer 1 Physical (bit synchronization, transmission), Layer 2 Data Link (framing, error detection, MAC), Layer 3 Network (routing), Layer 4 Transport (end-to-end, TCP/UDP).

given.aim: Identify which OSI layer/functionality pair is incorrectly stated

terms.meaning: Layer 3 — responsible for logical addressing and routing packets across networks
terms.example: IP protocol operates at the network layer; routers use it for packet forwarding
terms.connects: Option A states 'Network layer and Routing' — this is CORRECT

terms.meaning: Layer 2 — handles framing, MAC addressing, error detection, flow control between adjacent nodes
terms.example: Ethernet frame format with MAC addresses is a Data Link layer construct
terms.connects: Option B states 'Data Link Layer and Bit synchronization' — bit synchronization belongs to Physical layer (Layer 1), making this INCORRECT

terms.meaning: Layer 4 — provides end-to-end communication between processes on different hosts
terms.example: TCP and UDP operate at the transport layer providing process-to-process delivery
terms.connects: Option C states 'Transport layer and End-to-end process communication' — this is CORRECT

given.plan: Go through each option and verify whether the functionality correctly belongs to that OSI layer. Bit synchronization is a physical layer function, not data link layer, making option B the incorrect pair.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A: Network layer and Routing
steps.apply: The Network layer (Layer 3) is responsible for logical addressing and routing. Routers operate at this layer to determine paths for packets. This pairing is CORRECT.
steps.note: Routing protocols like OSPF, RIP, BGP all operate at the Network layer.

steps.title: Evaluate Option B: Data Link Layer and Bit synchronization
steps.apply: Bit synchronization (clock synchronization at the bit level) is a function of the Physical layer (Layer 1), not the Data Link layer (Layer 2). The Data Link layer handles framing, error detection, and MAC addressing. This pairing is INCORRECT.
steps.note: Bit synchronization ensures the receiver knows when each bit begins/ends — this is purely a physical layer concern.

steps.title: Verify Options C and D for completeness
steps.apply: Option C: Transport layer provides end-to-end process communication (TCP/UDP) — CORRECT. Option D: MAC sub-layer handles channel sharing (CSMA/CD, TDMA etc.) — CORRECT.
steps.note: Both C and D are correctly paired, confirming B is the only incorrect option.

solution.result: The INCORRECT pair is B: Data Link Layer and Bit synchronization (bit synchronization belongs to the Physical layer).

questions.formula_note: OSI model layer responsibilities: Physical layer handles bit synchronization and transmission; Data Link layer handles framing, error detection, and MAC; Network layer handles routing; Transport layer handles end-to-end process communication.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Field                         | Length in bits
------------------------------|---------------
P. UDP Header's Port Number   | I. 48
Q. Ethernet MAC Address       | II. 8
R. IPv6 Next Header           | III. 32
S. TCP Header's Sequence No.  | IV. 16

A. P-III, Q-IV, R-II, S-I
B. P-I, Q-IV, R-II, S-III
C. P-IV, Q-I, R-II, S-III
D. P-IV, Q-I, R-III, S-II
questions.to_find: Correct matching of network protocol header fields to their sizes in bits

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of specific header field sizes in common network protocols. You need to match each protocol field (UDP port number, Ethernet MAC address, IPv6 Next Header, TCP Sequence Number) with its correct size in bits (48, 8, 32, 16).

keywords.explain: A 16-bit field in the UDP header used to identify the source and destination application processes.
keywords.example: DNS uses port 53; HTTP uses port 80. Port numbers range from 0 to 65535, requiring 16 bits.

keywords.explain: A 48-bit (6-byte) hardware address uniquely identifying a network interface card on a LAN.
keywords.example: A MAC address like 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E has 6 bytes = 48 bits.

keywords.explain: An 8-bit field in the IPv6 header that identifies the type of header immediately following the IPv6 header (similar to IPv4 Protocol field).
keywords.example: Next Header value 6 means TCP follows; value 17 means UDP follows.

keywords.explain: A 32-bit field in the TCP header indicating the sequence number of the first data byte in the segment.
keywords.example: TCP sequence numbers can range from 0 to 2^32 - 1, requiring 32 bits for reliable ordered delivery.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 580 200' width='580' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='260' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='140' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Protocol Field</text><rect x='300' y='10' width='270' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='435' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Size in Bits</text><rect x='10' y='55' width='260' height='35' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='140' y='77' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P. UDP Port Number</text><rect x='300' y='55' width='270' height='35' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='435' y='77' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>IV = 16 bits (0-65535)</text><rect x='10' y='95' width='260' height='35' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='140' y='117' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Q. Ethernet MAC Address</text><rect x='300' y='95' width='270' height='35' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='435' y='117' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>I = 48 bits (6 bytes)</text><rect x='10' y='135' width='260' height='35' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='140' y='157' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>R. IPv6 Next Header</text><rect x='300' y='135' width='270' height='35' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='435' y='157' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>II = 8 bits (0-255)</text><rect x='10' y='175' width='260' height='35' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='140' y='197' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>S. TCP Sequence Number</text><rect x='300' y='175' width='270' height='35' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='435' y='197' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>III = 32 bits (2^32)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Match table: P (UDP Port Number) = 16 bits (IV), Q (Ethernet MAC) = 48 bits (I), R (IPv6 Next Header) = 8 bits (II), S (TCP Sequence No.) = 32 bits (III).

given.aim: Match each protocol header field to its correct bit-length

terms.term: P. UDP Header Port Number = 16 bits (IV)
terms.meaning: UDP uses 16-bit port numbers (0-65535) in both source and destination port fields
MAC addresses are 6 bytes = 48 bits, as defined in IEEE 802.3 standard
The IPv6 Next Header field is 8 bits wide, identifying the next protocol (same width as IPv4 Protocol field)
terms.example: TCP=6, UDP=17, ICMPv6=58 — all fit in 8 bits

given.plan: Recall the standard header field sizes: UDP port (16 bits), MAC address (48 bits), IPv6 Next Header (8 bits), TCP Sequence Number (32 bits). Then match P-IV, Q-I, R-II, S-III.

steps.title: Determine P: UDP Header Port Number
steps.apply: Port numbers go from 0 to 65535, which requires exactly 16 bits. So P = IV (16 bits).
steps.note: Both source and destination port fields in UDP are each 16 bits wide.

steps.title: Determine Q: Ethernet MAC Address
steps.apply: IEEE 802.3 specifies MAC addresses as 48 bits (6 octets). So Q = I (48 bits).
steps.note: MAC addresses are written as 6 groups of 2 hex digits (e.g. 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E).

steps.title: Determine R: IPv6 Next Header
steps.apply: The IPv6 Next Header is an 8-bit field (same role as IPv4 Protocol field). Values like 6 (TCP), 17 (UDP), 58 (ICMPv6) fit in 8 bits. So R = II (8 bits).
steps.note: IPv6 Next Header replaced the IPv4 Protocol field and has the same size.

steps.title: Determine S: TCP Sequence Number
steps.apply: TCP sequence numbers are 32-bit unsigned integers (0 to 2^32 - 1) for reliable ordered delivery. So S = III (32 bits).
steps.note: 32-bit sequence numbers allow TCP to wrap around after 4GB of data — sufficient for most connections.

solution.result: P-IV (16), Q-I (48), R-II (8), S-III (32). Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Standard network header field sizes: UDP/TCP port numbers are 16-bit; Ethernet MAC address is 48-bit; IPv6 Next Header is 8-bit; TCP Sequence Number is 32-bit — all defined by IEEE 802.3 and IETF RFCs.

questions.question:
The address resolution protocol (ARP) is used for:

A. Finding the IP address from the DNS
B. Finding the IP address of the default gateway
C. Finding the IP address that corresponds to a MAC address
D. Finding the MAC address that corresponds to an IP address
questions.answer: D. Finding the MAC address that corresponds to an IP address
questions.to_find: The primary purpose of the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)

understand.plain: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) is a network protocol used to translate a known IP address into the corresponding MAC (hardware) address on a local network. When a host wants to send data to another host on the same subnet, it knows the destination's IP address but needs the MAC address to construct the Ethernet frame. ARP resolves this by broadcasting a query and getting the MAC address back.

keywords.explain: A protocol that maps a 32-bit IP address to a 48-bit MAC address on a local network. It operates between the Network and Data Link layers.
keywords.example: If host A wants to send to IP 192.168.1.5, it broadcasts 'Who has 192.168.1.5?' and that host replies with its MAC address.

keywords.explain: A hardware address (48 bits) permanently assigned to a network interface card, used at the Data Link layer for local delivery.
keywords.example: 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E is a typical MAC address format.

keywords.explain: A table maintained by each host mapping IP addresses to MAC addresses, to avoid repeated ARP broadcasts.
keywords.example: After resolving 192.168.1.5 -> 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E, this mapping is stored in the ARP cache for reuse.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 580 160' width='580' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='50' width='140' height='70' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Host A</text><text x='80' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>IP: 192.168.1.4</text><rect x='430' y='50' width='140' height='70' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='500' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Host B</text><text x='500' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>IP: 192.168.1.5</text><line x1='150' y1='75' x2='430' y2='75' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='290' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>ARP Request (broadcast)</text><text x='290' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>'Who has 192.168.1.5?'</text><line x1='430' y1='100' x2='150' y2='100' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='290' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>ARP Reply (unicast)</text><text x='290' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>'I am 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E'</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: ARP process: Host A broadcasts 'Who has IP 192.168.1.5?' to the network. Host B replies with its MAC address 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E. Host A now knows the MAC address corresponding to the target IP.

given.aim: Identify the correct description of what ARP does

terms.meaning: The logical 32-bit address of the destination host that the sender already knows
terms.connects: ARP takes this as input to find the MAC address

terms.meaning: The 48-bit physical hardware address needed to deliver the Ethernet frame on the local network
terms.connects: ARP's output: maps the known IP to this physical address

terms.meaning: A broadcast message sent to all hosts on the subnet asking 'Who has IP address X?'
terms.example: ARP Request: Target IP = 192.168.1.5, Target MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (broadcast)
terms.connects: The host with IP X responds with its MAC address

given.plan: Recall that ARP translates IP addresses to MAC addresses (not the other way around — that's RARP/InARP). Eliminate options A (DNS function), B (configuration/DHCP), C (reverse: MAC to IP — that's RARP). Option D correctly states IP to MAC.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply: A: 'Finding IP from DNS' — DNS maps hostnames to IP addresses, not ARP's function. B: 'Finding IP of default gateway' — this is done via DHCP or manual configuration, not ARP. C: 'Finding IP corresponding to a MAC address' — this describes RARP (Reverse ARP), not ARP.
steps.note: RARP is the reverse of ARP: given a MAC, find the IP. ARP does MAC from IP.

steps.title: Confirm option D
steps.apply: D: 'Finding the MAC address that corresponds to an IP address' — this exactly describes ARP. The host broadcasts 'Who has IP X?' and receives a reply with the corresponding MAC address. This MAC is then used in the Ethernet frame header for local delivery.
steps.note: ARP operates on the local subnet only. For remote destinations, ARP is used to find the MAC of the default gateway, not the final destination.

solution.result: ARP is used for finding the MAC address that corresponds to an IP address. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: ARP (RFC 826) resolves IP-to-MAC address mappings on local networks using broadcast requests and unicast replies. The reverse operation (MAC to IP) is performed by RARP or InARP.
